name: musicBottles
short_description: A tangible interface in which uncorking glass bottles releases individual instrumental tracks of a musical composition.
long_description: musicBottles is a tangible user interface from the MIT Media Lab's Tangible Media Group consisting of a custom table and three corked glass bottles that 'contain' the violin, cello, and piano tracks of Edouard Lalo's Piano Trio in C Minor. Custom electromagnetic tags embedded in the bottles let the table wirelessly identify each one and sense when its cork is removed, at which point the corresponding instrument becomes audible while colored light is rear-projected onto the translucent surface to reflect pitch and volume. It matters as physical AI / interaction design because it exemplifies the transparent, ambient interface ideal — using the everyday, emotional affordance of opening a bottle as a minimal, embodied control for digital media, weaving computation into familiar objects rather than screens.
ai_note: The system uses electromagnetic tag sensing and audio playback with no machine learning or AI components.

images.caption: The musicBottles table with three corked glass bottles, each containing an instrumental track that plays when uncorked.
images.alt_text: Three glass bottles arranged on a glowing translucent table; uncorking a bottle releases its instrument's audio.

sources.title: musicBottles
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab Tangible Media Group

sources.title: Bottles as a minimal interface to access digital information
sources.publisher: CHI '01 Extended Abstracts on Human Factors in Computing Systems, ACM

interactions.title: Uncork an instrument
interactions.description: Lift the cork off one bottle on the lit table and that instrument's part of Lalo's Piano Trio in C Minor becomes audible; recork it and the violin, cello, or piano drops back out of the mix.

interactions.title: Conduct the trio by hand
interactions.description: With three bottles on the stage, a person opens and closes them in different combinations to build the piece up or strip it down to a solo, arranging the music with the everyday gesture of opening a bottle rather than any screen or control.

interactions.title: Watch the music in light
interactions.description: As tracks play, a pattern of colored light is rear-projected up through the translucent tabletop, shifting with the pitch and volume of the sound so the bottles appear to glow in time with what you hear.

interactions.title: A bottle that holds the weather
interactions.description: In the design that seeded the project, Ishii imagined bottles delivering weather, news, and music to his mother: opening one to a chirp of birdsong meant a fine day, while another held the sound of a jazz trio.
interactions.source_label: MIT Media Lab - Ishii's living legacy

interactions.title: Open a bottle to a jazz trio
interactions.description: Beyond the classical trio, the bottles were filled with the recording of a jazz ensemble, so uncorking each one releases a single player and lets a listener pull the band apart instrument by instrument.
interactions.source_label: MIT Media Lab - Ishii's living legacy

interactions.title: A scent-bottle inbox for messages
interactions.description: Following the weather-bottle idea, a small set of perfume-style bottles on a nightstand could each hold a voice from a particular family member, so uncorking one at the end of the day plays back a short message they left, grounded in the same tag-and-cork sensing.

interactions.title: Pour out a soundscape for a room
interactions.description: Using the cork-open sensing to fade ambient audio in and out, a cafe could set bottles on each table so guests open one to release birdsong, rain, or a string quartet into their corner without touching a phone or speaker.
